title: Video game piracy

infobox.value: is the unauthorized copying and distributing of [video game](/wiki/Video_game "Video game") software, and is a form of [copyright infringement](/wiki/Copyright_infringement "Copyright infringement"). It is often cited as a major problem that [video game publishers](/wiki/Video_game_publisher "Video game publisher") face when distributing their products, due to the ease of being able to distribute games for free, via torrenting or websites offering [direct download links](/wiki/Direct_download_link "Direct download link").[[1]](#cite_note-raliff-1) Right holders generally attempt to counter piracy of their products by enforcing the [Digital Millennium Copyright Act](/wiki/Digital_Millennium_Copyright_Act "Digital Millennium Copyright Act"), though this has never been totally successful.[[1]](#cite_note-raliff-1) Digital distribution of pirated games has historically occurred on [bulletin board systems](/wiki/Bulletin_board_system "Bulletin board system") (BBS), and more recently via decentralized [peer-to-peer](/wiki/Peer-to-peer "Peer-to-peer") torrenting.[[2]](#cite_note-wasiak-2)[[3]](#cite_note-moshirnia-3) In terms of physical distribution, [China](/wiki/China "China"), [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia") and [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam") are known for major manufacturing and distribution centers for pirated game copies,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] while [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong") and [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore") are major importers.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Form of copyright infringement

**Video game piracy** is the unauthorized copying and distributing of [video game](/wiki/Video_game "Video game") software, and is a form of [copyright infringement](/wiki/Copyright_infringement "Copyright infringement"). It is often cited as a major problem that [video game publishers](/wiki/Video_game_publisher "Video game publisher") face when distributing their products, due to the ease of being able to distribute games for free, via torrenting or websites offering [direct download links](/wiki/Direct_download_link "Direct download link").[[1]](#cite_note-raliff-1) Right holders generally attempt to counter piracy of their products by enforcing the [Digital Millennium Copyright Act](/wiki/Digital_Millennium_Copyright_Act "Digital Millennium Copyright Act"), though this has never been totally successful.[[1]](#cite_note-raliff-1) Digital distribution of pirated games has historically occurred on [bulletin board systems](/wiki/Bulletin_board_system "Bulletin board system") (BBS), and more recently via decentralized [peer-to-peer](/wiki/Peer-to-peer "Peer-to-peer") torrenting.[[2]](#cite_note-wasiak-2)[[3]](#cite_note-moshirnia-3) In terms of physical distribution, [China](/wiki/China "China"), [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia") and [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam") are known for major manufacturing and distribution centers for pirated game copies,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] while [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong") and [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore") are major importers.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## History and culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_piracy&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History and culture")]

See also: [Warez scene](/wiki/Warez_scene "Warez scene")

As the personal computer rose to prominence in the mid to late 1970s, so too did the tendency to copy video games onto [floppy disks](/wiki/Floppy_disk "Floppy disk") and [cassette tapes](/wiki/Cassette_tape#Institutional_duplication "Cassette tape"), and share pirated copies by hand.[[5]](#cite_note-reunanen_wasiak_botz-5) Piracy networks can be traced back to the mid-1980s, with infrastructure changes resulting from the [Bell System breakup](/wiki/Breakup_of_the_Bell_System "Breakup of the Bell System") serving as a major catalyst. Video game trading circles began to emerge in the years following, with networks of computers, connected via [modem](/wiki/Modem "Modem") to [long-distance telephone](/wiki/Long-distance_calling "Long-distance calling") lines, transmitting the contents of floppy discs.[[2]](#cite_note-wasiak-2) These trading circles became colloquially known as the Warez scene, with the term "[warez](/wiki/Warez "Warez")" being an informal bastardization of "software".[[5]](#cite_note-reunanen_wasiak_botz-5)

In the 1980s, [crack intros](/wiki/Crack_intro "Crack intro") began appearing on pirated games. Preceding the booting of the actual game, these windows would contain the monikers of those who created the pirated copy, along with any messages they wanted to add. Beginning as simple text, the presentation of these crack intros gradually grew more complex, with windows featuring [GIFs](/wiki/GIF "GIF"), music, and colorful designs.[[5]](#cite_note-reunanen_wasiak_botz-5)

With the rise of [bulletin board systems](/wiki/Bulletin_board_system "Bulletin board system") throughout the 1980s and into the 1990s, the sharing of pirated video games took a centralized form. By connecting personal computers to telephone modems, and dialing a number to a dedicated [server](/wiki/Server_(computing) "Server (computing)"), members of the Warez scene could share their copies of video games.[[2]](#cite_note-wasiak-2) Furthermore, because this system preceded the rise of the consumer-level [internet](/wiki/Internet "Internet"), it could go relatively unnoticed. However, with the rise of peer-to-peer torrenting, and notably with the release of [BitTorrent](/wiki/BitTorrent "BitTorrent") in 2001, this BBS format of video game piracy began to decline.[[2]](#cite_note-wasiak-2) Nowadays, torrenting pirated games remains the popular choice among those who engage in piracy. Efforts to thwart illegal torrenting have historically failed, because its decentralized nature makes it effectively impossible to totally dismantle.[[3]](#cite_note-moshirnia-3)

## Anti-piracy measures

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_piracy&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Anti-piracy measures")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e1/Anti_DRM_in_dhaka-1.jpg/250px-Anti_DRM_in_dhaka-1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Anti_DRM_in_dhaka-1.jpg)

A 2016 protest in Dhaka against DRM

The use of [copy protection](/wiki/Copy_protection "Copy protection") has been a commonplace throughout the history of video games. Early copy protection measures for video games included [Lenslok](/wiki/Lenslok "Lenslok"), [code wheels](/wiki/Code_wheel "Code wheel"), and special instructions that would require the player to own the manual.[[6]](#cite_note-eurogamer-6)[[7]](#cite_note-gamesradar-7) Several early copy protection measures have been criticized for both their ineffectiveness at preventing piracy, and their inconvenience to the player.[[6]](#cite_note-eurogamer-6)[[7]](#cite_note-gamesradar-7) One of the most typical means of copy protection is to assign a [serial key](/wiki/Product_key "Product key") to each legitimate copy of the game, so that it can only be activated by entering the serial. However, this is often circumvented via [software cracking](/wiki/Software_cracking "Software cracking"), or through the use of a [keygen](/wiki/Keygen "Keygen").

More recent attempts to hinder piracy have included [digital rights management](/wiki/Digital_rights_management "Digital rights management") tools.[[3]](#cite_note-moshirnia-3) A form of this is the sale of games on [digital distribution](/wiki/Digital_distribution "Digital distribution") platforms, such as the [Epic Games Store](/wiki/Epic_Games_Store "Epic Games Store"), [Blizzard](/wiki/Blizzard_Entertainment "Blizzard Entertainment")'s [Battle.net](/wiki/Battle.net "Battle.net"), and [Steam](/wiki/Steam_(service) "Steam (service)"). Steam offers proprietary features such as accelerated downloads, [cloud](/wiki/Cloud_computing "Cloud computing") [saves](/wiki/Saved_game "Saved game"), automatic [patching](/wiki/Patch_(computing) "Patch (computing)"), and [achievements](/wiki/Achievement_(video_gaming) "Achievement (video gaming)") that pirated copies do not have. The purpose of these features is to make piracy look less attractive, and to incentivize the legitimate purchase of games. [Gabe Newell](/wiki/Gabe_Newell "Gabe Newell"), creator of Steam, has stated that creating "service value" discourages piracy more than adding additional DRM.[[8]](#cite_note-8) Some games, such as *[Grand Theft Auto IV](/wiki/Grand_Theft_Auto_IV "Grand Theft Auto IV")*, use DRM that negatively alters gameplay, if it detects that the game is an illegitimate copy. In *GTA IV*'s case, it disables the brakes on cars and gives the camera an amplified drunk effect, making gameplay much harder, thus creating an incentive to legitimately purchase the game.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Sometimes, games require online authentication or have [always-on DRM](/wiki/Always-on_DRM "Always-on DRM").[[1]](#cite_note-raliff-1) A notable incident concerning always-on DRM took place in 2021, surrounding the [Windows](/wiki/Windows_10 "Windows 10") release of *[Crash Bandicoot 4: It's About Time](/wiki/Crash_Bandicoot_4:_It%27s_About_Time "Crash Bandicoot 4: It's About Time")*. Without a constant internet connection, the game's DRM disallows any play at all, even in [single-player](/wiki/Single-player_video_game "Single-player video game"), which naturally drew ire.[[11]](#cite_note-11) However, the Warez scene cracked this DRM feature almost immediately. A cracked version of *Crash Bandicoot 4*, stripped of this DRM, began circulating online just one day after its official release.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

## Emulation and piracy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_piracy&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Emulation and piracy")]

Historically, video game companies have blamed [video game emulators](/wiki/Video_game_console_emulator "Video game console emulator") for piracy, despite the fact that anyone can create their own legal [ROM image](/wiki/ROM_image "ROM image") from the original media.[[13]](#cite_note-13) Concerning this demonization of emulators, video game historian [Frank Cifaldi](/wiki/Frank_Cifaldi "Frank Cifaldi") attributed it to the [Connectix Virtual Game Station](/wiki/Connectix_Virtual_Game_Station "Connectix Virtual Game Station"). Released in 1999, this commercial emulator enabled the play of [Sony PlayStation](/wiki/PlayStation_(console) "PlayStation (console)") games on [Macintosh](/wiki/Macintosh "Macintosh") computers. However, it was easily cracked by hackers to play pirated [ISO images](/wiki/ISO_image "ISO image") of [PlayStation](/wiki/PlayStation_(console) "PlayStation (console)") games, leading to a lawsuit from [Sony](/wiki/Sony "Sony"). Companies continued to fear that emulators would encourage piracy.[[14]](#cite_note-ars_orland-14) This has created a long-running debate over emulation, since many [out-of-print](/wiki/Out_of_print "Out of print") video games can only be played via ROM, making emulators the only replacement for [defunct](/wiki/End-of-life_(product) "End-of-life (product)") video game consoles. Additionally, modern [remasters](/wiki/Video-game_remaster "Video-game remaster") and [remakes](/wiki/Video_game_remake "Video game remake") can significantly alter a game, sometimes in a manner that changes the entire gameplay experience.[[14]](#cite_note-ars_orland-14) For example, *[Crash Bandicoot N. Sane Trilogy](/wiki/Crash_Bandicoot_N._Sane_Trilogy "Crash Bandicoot N. Sane Trilogy")* had a rewritten [physics engine](/wiki/Physics_engine "Physics engine"), requiring players to make more precise jumps.[[15]](#cite_note-15) Such gameplay changes give importance to emulators, which may be able to run the original game.

Some companies still consider emulators copyright-infringing. In 2017, [Atlus](/wiki/Atlus "Atlus") attempted to take down the [Patreon](/wiki/Patreon "Patreon") page of a [PlayStation 3](/wiki/PlayStation_3 "PlayStation 3") emulator, [RPCS3](/wiki/RPCS3 "RPCS3"), arguing that the ability to play *[Persona 5](/wiki/Persona_5 "Persona 5")* on it made it illegal software. However, Patreon disagreed with the company's stance, and allowed the page to remain, as long as references to *Persona 5* were removed.[[16]](#cite_note-16) Furthermore, [Nintendo](/wiki/Nintendo "Nintendo") has taken decisive action against the emulation of its games in recent years. In 2018, the company sued a handful of large ROM sites, forcing them to remove ROMs of their older console games, for what they called “brazen and mass-scale infringement of Nintendo’s [intellectual property](/wiki/Intellectual_property "Intellectual property") rites [sic]".[[17]](#cite_note-17) The distribution of copied ROM files online is illegal, but this move by Nintendo was interpreted by the emulation scene as an attack on the emulation of older games.[[18]](#cite_note-18) In November 2020, Nintendo issued a [cease and desist](/wiki/Cease_and_desist "Cease and desist") order to [The Big House](/wiki/The_Big_House_(tournament) "The Big House (tournament)"), an annual [Super Smash Bros](/wiki/Super_Smash_Bros. "Super Smash Bros.") tournament. The Big House ran SSB games on the [Dolphin emulator](/wiki/Dolphin_(emulator) "Dolphin (emulator)"), and it was the addition of the mod Slippi, which enabled online play, that caught Nintendo's attention.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

Despite longstanding criticism of emulators in the game industry, companies themselves have used emulation to run commercial games. Nintendo operated the [Virtual Console](/wiki/Virtual_Console "Virtual Console"), which allowed people to buy and play certain games via emulation.[[14]](#cite_note-ars_orland-14) In 2017, the [PlayStation 4](/wiki/PlayStation_4 "PlayStation 4") was found to contain a functioning internal [PSP](/wiki/PlayStation_Portable "PlayStation Portable") emulator. Hackers discovered that *[PaRappa the Rapper Remastered](/wiki/PaRappa_the_Rapper_Remastered "PaRappa the Rapper Remastered")* was actually the 2007 PSP version, running with upscaled [textures](/wiki/Texture_mapping "Texture mapping"). This emulator was later [reverse-engineered](/wiki/Reverse_engineering "Reverse engineering") and used to play other PSP games.[[20]](#cite_note-20) Additionally, [Microsoft](/wiki/Microsoft "Microsoft")'s [Xbox One](/wiki/Xbox_One "Xbox One") console uses a [proprietary](/wiki/Proprietary_software "Proprietary software") emulator in order to play games released for the original [Xbox](/wiki/Xbox_(console) "Xbox (console)"), and the [Xbox 360](/wiki/Xbox_360 "Xbox 360").[[21]](#cite_note-21)

## Console modding and piracy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_piracy&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Console modding and piracy")]

See also: [Modding § Video game consoles](/wiki/Modding#Video_game_consoles "Modding")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/Xenium_ICE_in_XBOX.JPG/250px-Xenium_ICE_in_XBOX.JPG)](/wiki/File:Xenium_ICE_in_XBOX.JPG)

The motherboard of an original Xbox, with a Xenium ICE modchip installed

Modded consoles have been cited as an avenue for video game piracy, both by allowing unauthorized copies to be played, and by circumventing DRM. Legal [homebrew video games](/wiki/Homebrew_(video_games) "Homebrew (video games)") and [backup](/wiki/Backup "Backup") copies can be played on modified systems, but the argument of piracy remains. A notable example of console modding is the [original Microsoft Xbox](/wiki/Xbox_modding "Xbox modding"), of which tutorials still exist to this day. By introducing [softmod](/wiki/Softmod "Softmod") installation files via one of the four controller ports, players could overhaul the system [UI](/wiki/User_interface "User interface"), install and play games directly off the internal [hard disk drive](/wiki/Hard_disk_drive "Hard disk drive"), and even play TV shows and movies on the console.[[22]](#cite_note-ellisworkshop-22) Some softmods even re-enable online play on the original Xbox, despite the first incarnation of [Xbox Live](/wiki/Xbox_Live "Xbox Live") having shut down in 2010.[[22]](#cite_note-ellisworkshop-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23) A handful of hardmod techniques also exist for the original Xbox, enabling hardware additions and upgrades to non-stock components.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

Companies such as Nintendo have coordinated with [law enforcement agencies](/wiki/Law_enforcement_agency "Law enforcement agency") to track down and [seize](/wiki/Search_and_seizure "Search and seizure") [modchips](/wiki/Modchip "Modchip") for their consoles, such as in the 2007 [Operation Tangled Web](/wiki/Operation_Tangled_Web "Operation Tangled Web").[[25]](#cite_note-25) The same year, Nintendo also began a crackdown against merchants of [R4](/wiki/R4_cartridge "R4 cartridge") [flash cartridges](/wiki/Flash_cartridge "Flash cartridge"), which could be used to play pirated ROMs on the [Nintendo DS](/wiki/Nintendo_DS "Nintendo DS"). In 2009, the device was declared illegal to sell or import into Japan, among other countries.[[26]](#cite_note-26) In 2018, a man was arrested in the United Kingdom, for allegedly running a Nintendo Switch modding operation out of his house.[[27]](#cite_note-27) Furthermore, in 2020, two prominent members of the hacking group [Team Xecuter](/wiki/Team_Xecuter "Team Xecuter") were arrested and [indicted](/wiki/Indictment "Indictment") by the [Department of Justice](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Justice "United States Department of Justice"). The indictment detailed their creation of modded consoles, which were "designed to be circumvention devices that had the purpose of allowing users to play pirated ROMs."[[28]](#cite_note-28)
